location_information.location_address_2: Irrigated pasture parcel: NW 1/4 of Section 12, Township 4N, Range 57W, Morgan County, CO (no structures)

coverage__claims_history.explain_claims_incidents:
In July 2022 (exact date of loss: 2022-07-18), a severe hail and windstorm moved through our area and caused damage to the metal roofing on our main equipment shed and partially to the siding on the west side of the small hay barn. We filed a property claim under our existing farm policy with Great Plains Mutual. The adjuster inspected the damage on 2022-07-22. The cause of loss was documented as wind-driven hail and straight-line winds associated with a severe thunderstorm. 

Total paid amount by the carrier was approximately $18,750 after deductible. This covered replacing several roof panels on the equipment shed, repairing flashing, and repainting and re-siding a portion of the hay barn wall. We obtained two estimates from local contractors (High Country Builders and Morgan Ag Construction); the final invoice from Morgan Ag Construction came in close to the adjuster’s estimate. There were no injuries, no liability claims, and no damage to livestock or machinery. We took several steps afterward to reduce future damage, including trimming back a row of older cottonwood trees that had been dropping branches and adding additional roof fasteners on the new panels as recommended by the contractor.

Prior to this 2022 incident, our last claim was more than 8 years ago (a minor lightning-related electrical issue in 2015 that did not involve this current property configuration and is outside the requested 5-year window).

business__use_of_premises.describe_activities_and_revenues:
In addition to our primary cow-calf and small-scale hay operation, we have a few related but distinct activities on the property that generate supplemental income:

1) CUSTOM HAY BALING SERVICES FOR NEIGHBORING FARMS
We own a mid-sized round baler and a smaller square baler, and during peak season (typically June through early September), I provide custom baling services for two neighboring farms and one small acreage owner. The work is mostly cutting, raking, and baling grass/alfalfa mix on their fields; we do not transport the hay off their property except occasionally to move bales from the field to their on-site storage. Annual gross revenue from this custom baling averages between $14,000 and $18,000; for the last calendar year it was approximately $15,600. This is done on a per-acre or per-bale basis depending on the neighbor’s preference, and is heavily weather-dependent. All work is performed by myself and my adult son, with no outside employees.

2) SMALL-SCALE DIRECT EGG SALES
My wife keeps a flock of approximately 40 laying hens in a fenced coop and run behind our main house. We sell farm-fresh eggs directly to local families and a small café in town. Sales are conducted either from a self-serve honor-system cooler at the end of our driveway (cash box and Venmo sign) or by pre-arranged weekly deliveries into Fort Morgan. Average production is 20–25 dozen eggs per week in peak season and 10–15 dozen in winter. We sell at roughly $4.50 per dozen to households and $3.75 per dozen to the café. Annual gross revenue from egg sales over the last year was about $4,800. We do not do any on-site processing beyond basic washing and packaging into cartons.

3) SEASONAL PUMPKIN PATCH / U-PICK EVENT (VERY SMALL SCALE)
Each fall, usually the first three weekends in October, we open a small section of the property (about 1.5 acres) as a u-pick pumpkin patch for local families. We plant pumpkins and a few decorative gourds in late spring. We advertise informally via Facebook and flyers at the local feed store and coffee shop. Visitors park in a mowed grass area near our driveway and walk to the patch. We provide wheelbarrows and basic signage, but there are no rides, bounce houses, or large events—just families picking pumpkins, taking photos, and occasionally feeding our docile cow-calf pairs from behind a fence with feed we provide. We charge by the pumpkin size and variety, and we typically gross between $3,000 and $4,500 each season; last year’s total was roughly $3,700. We limit attendance to daytime hours on weekends, require children to be supervised by an adult, and post basic safety rules at the entrance.

4) MINOR HOBBY PRODUCE SALES
On a very small scale, we also sell surplus produce (primarily tomatoes, cucumbers, and sweet corn) from a roadside table and through word-of-mouth to neighbors. This is not a major revenue source—last year we estimate less than $1,200 in total sales. Produce is grown in our garden and a small high-tunnel near the house. We do not can or process foods for sale; everything is sold fresh.

Combined, these non-equine agricultural side activities generate approximately $25,000 in gross annual revenue in a typical year. These activities are all conducted on our property at the above addresses, involve only family labor (no hired employees), and are closely tied to our primary agricultural operation rather than being unrelated commercial businesses.

leased_portions_of_property.describe_leased_portions:
We lease out approximately 25 acres of our 40-acre irrigated pasture parcel (Location 2 described above) to a neighboring rancher, Miguel Hernandez, under a written grazing agreement that runs from May 1 through October 31 each year. The leased portion consists of fenced, flood-irrigated grass pasture with a small seasonal stock pond. There are no permanent buildings or structures on the leased section—only perimeter fencing, a couple of metal gates, and a portable stock tank that the lessee provides.

Under the lease, Miguel runs 18–22 cow-calf pairs on the pasture during the season, subject to rotational grazing guidelines we agree on each spring. He is responsible for his own cattle health, vaccinations, and day-to-day care. We maintain the perimeter fencing and irrigation ditches, and we periodically check the water supply and gates. Rent is paid on a per-head-per-month basis, and in the last full grazing season we received approximately $6,200 in total lease payments.

We have a clause in the lease requiring Miguel to carry his own farm liability insurance and to name us as an additional insured for his grazing operations on our land. The lease also prohibits any subleasing, on-site events, or activities unrelated to routine cattle grazing (no boarding of outside animals, no commercial hunting, etc.).
